explanation:
TRUE POSITIVE. The exploit chain is fully confirmed in source code:

1. `BI.renameBox` (LibraryBI.sol:84-85) checks name uniqueness only within the single Emap passed to it. When BoxMgr.renameBox tries _boxes first (fails — old name not found), then tries _inactive (succeeds — new name not found in _inactive, even though it exists in _boxes), it creates a cross-map name collision: both _boxes and _inactive now contain the same nameKey.

2. `rotateBox` then calls `BI.addBoxNoCheck(dst, nameKey, info)` (LibraryBI.sol:51-57) which blindly overwrites `dst.idxByName[nameKey]` with the new index. When dst (_boxes) already has that nameKey mapped to the original entry (boxProxy A), the overwrite makes the new entry (boxProxy B) canonical while leaving boxProxy A as a permanently orphaned element in the values array — unreachable by name-based lookup, unremovable by name-based removal.

3. All subsequent name-based operations including Vault withdrawals via `push(boxName, ...)` (BoxMgr.sol:414) are misrouted to boxProxy B. Funds held in boxProxy A are effectively stranded with no recovery path short of a contract upgrade.

Requires Agent role (a designated governance role, not unprivileged), but any single compromised or malicious Agent account can execute both operations autonomously.
